question_title: Will Sergio Fajardo publicly endorse a candidate in Colombia's 2026 presidential runoff before June 14, 2026?
summary: Sergio Fajardo has a deeply established pattern of refusing to endorse in Colombian presidential runoffs (blank votes in both 2018 and 2022). As of June 3-5, 2026, he is emphatically and publicly refusing to endorse either De la Espriella or Cepeda, stating he is 'not negotiating with anyone,' that blank vote is 'a legitimate option,' and that his decálogo is not for negotiation. His statements go beyond hedging — they represent a categorical stance consistent with his political identity. While the extremely close runoff margin (2.84pp) creates enormous pressure, this dynamic also existed in 2018 when Fajardo held 23.7% of votes and was an even bigger kingmaker — yet he still refused. The question's strict resolution criteria (requiring unconditional named endorsement, filtering out conditional statements and blank vote advocacy) further reduce the probability. I estimate ~9% chance of YES, accounting for tail scenarios like a democratic crisis or one candidate dramatically adopting Fajardo's platform that could force his hand in the remaining 9 days.

factors.description: Strong historical pattern: Fajardo cast blank votes in both 2018 and 2022 runoffs, 0 endorsements in 2 prior opportunities. This is his established political identity.

Current emphatic refusal (June 3-5): 'No estoy negociando nada con nadie,' 'los votos no son de los dirigentes,' 'el voto en blanco es una opción legítima,' decálogo explicitly not for negotiation. Multiple direct quotes from El Colombiano, La FM, Pulzo.

Principled identity framing: Fajardo frames non-endorsement as core centrist identity, not tactical delay. His decálogo was presented as principles, not conditions: 'Yo no estoy presentando estos puntos para negociar a nadie.'

Very close runoff (2.84pp margin between De la Espriella at 43.74% and Cepeda at 40.90%) creates enormous pressure from both campaigns, media, and analysts for endorsement. Fajardo's 1M votes identified as 'potentially pivotal' by Edelman.
Highly polarized left-right matchup could push Fajardo to break pattern if he perceives existential threat to democracy from one candidate.

Only 9 days remain (June 5-14), and conditional statements don't count per fine print — only unconditional named endorsement qualifies. Even Fajardo's 'I'll decide whether to announce my vote' suggests he may never announce.

Question fine print is strict: conditional statements, criticizing one candidate without naming the other, blank vote advocacy all resolve NO. This filters out many possible Fajardo actions short of a full endorsement.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 9% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**

1. **Historical pattern is very strong:** Fajardo has NEVER endorsed a runoff candidate in Colombian presidential elections — blank vote in both 2018 and 2022.

2. **Current stance is emphatic and categorical (as of June 3-5):**
   - "I'm not negotiating anything with anyone"
   - "Votes don't belong to leaders, they belong to citizens"
   - "Blank vote is a legitimate option"
   - His decálogo is explicitly "not for negotiating"
   - When pressed on who he'll vote for: "I'll vote for whoever I want, and I'll decide whether to announce or not"

3. **Strict resolution criteria** filter out anything short of an unconditional, public, named endorsement — conditional statements, criticizing one candidate, and blank vote advocacy all resolve NO.

4. **Only 9 days remain** (June 5-14) for Fajardo to reverse his deeply held, publicly stated, repeatedly affirmed position.

5. **The ~9% tail probability** accounts for the unprecedented pressure of a razor-thin 2.84pp margin and the possibility of a dramatic development (democratic crisis, candidate concession to Fajardo's platform) that could break his pattern.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Sergio Fajardo publicly endorse a candidate in Colombia's 2026 presidential runoff before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43781)
[Four Takeaways from Colombia's First-Round Vote: De la Espriella, Cepeda Head to Runoff](https://www.as-coa.org/articles/four-takeaways-colombias-first-round-vote-de-la-espriella-cepeda-head-runoff)

[Fajardo aún no revela apoyo en segunda vuelta: “los votos no son de los dirigentes”](https://www.elcolombiano.com/colombia/politica/elecciones-2026-sergio-fajardo-revela-apoyo-segunda-vuelta-GO37284064)
[Sergio Fajardo: “El voto en blanco es una opción legítima”](https://www.lafm.com.co/politica/sergio-fajardo-voto-en-blanco-es-una-opcion-legitima-entrevista-401364)

["¿Por quién va a votar en segunda vuelta?": Néstor Morales dejó frío a Fajardo, en Blu](https://www.pulzo.com/elecciones-colombia-2026/sergio-fajardo-mananas-blu-sobre-segunda-vuelta-presidencial-PP5205399)

[Fajardo cierra puertas a alianzas para la segunda vuelta: “No estoy negociando nada con nadie”](https://www.elcolombiano.com/especiales/elecciones-2026/fajardo-descarta-alianzas-para-segunda-vuelta-EB37364981)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if, between May 31, 2026 and June 14, 2026 (inclusive, Colombia local time), Sergio Fajardo publicly states support, endorsement, or a declared vote intention for one of the two candidates contesting Colombia's June 21, 2026 presidential runoff, as reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as El Tiempo, El Espectador, Semana, La Silla Vacía, Reuters, or AP. The statement must be made by Fajardo himself (in person, in writing, on his verified social media, or in a recorded interview) and must clearly name a runoff candidate he is supporting or will vote for.

Otherwise (including if Fajardo explicitly declines to endorse, calls for a blank/null vote, endorses abstention, makes only conditional statements without naming a candidate, or remains silent), the question resolves NO.

If no runoff is held (e.g., a first-round winner exceeds 50% or the runoff is cancelled/postponed past June 14), the question resolves NO.
fine_print:
- Endorsing a blank vote ("voto en blanco"), abstention, or null vote does NOT count as endorsing a candidate.
- Statements that merely criticize one runoff candidate without naming the other as the preferred choice do NOT count.
- Conditional statements (e.g., "I could support X if they commit to Y") do NOT count unless Fajardo declares the conditions met and confirms his support for a named candidate.
- If Fajardo himself is one of the two runoff candidates, the question resolves NO (he cannot meaningfully endorse himself for the runoff in the sense intended).
- The endorsement must be made publicly; private communications reported second-hand do not count unless Fajardo himself confirms them publicly.

factors.description: Strong historical pattern: Fajardo cast blank votes in both 2018 and 2022 runoffs, 0 endorsements in 2 prior opportunities
Current emphatic refusal as of June 3-5: explicitly refusing to endorse, not negotiating, says blank vote is legitimate, decálogo is not for negotiation
Principled identity framing: Fajardo frames non-endorsement as core centrist identity, not tactical delay. His brand IS independence.
Very close runoff (2.84pp margin) creates enormous pressure from both campaigns and media for endorsement, plus 'kingmaker' narrative
Highly polarized left-right matchup could push Fajardo to break pattern if he perceives existential threat to democracy from one candidate
Only 9 days remain (June 5-14), and conditional statements don't count per fine print — only unconditional named endorsement qualifies
Question fine print is strict: conditional statements, criticizing one candidate without naming the other, blank vote calls all resolve NO

input.anchor: Historical base rate: 0% (0 out of 2 prior runoffs resulted in Fajardo endorsement). Laplace-smoothed: ~25% (1/4). However, given his current emphatic refusal, anchoring lower at ~15%.
input.assessment:
Fajardo has a deeply established pattern of refusing to endorse in Colombian presidential runoffs (blank vote in 2018 and 2022). As of June 3-5, 2026, he is emphatically and publicly refusing to endorse either De la Espriella or Cepeda. His statements are not hedged or ambiguous — he is categorically stating he is not negotiating with anyone, that votes belong to citizens not leaders, and that blank vote is a legitimate option. His "Decálogo del millón de votos" was explicitly presented as principles, NOT as conditions for negotiation.

The main force toward YES is the extraordinary pressure created by a razor-thin 2.84pp margin in a highly polarized left-right runoff. Both campaigns covet his million votes. However, this same dynamic existed in prior elections (especially 2018, when Fajardo had 23.7% of first-round votes and was a much bigger kingmaker), and he still refused.

The question's strict resolution criteria further reduce the YES probability: conditional statements don't count, criticizing one candidate without naming the other doesn't count, and blank vote advocacy resolves NO. Even if Fajardo softens his stance somewhat, he would need to make an unconditional, public, named endorsement — which would be a dramatic departure from both his current position and his political identity.

I estimate ~9% probability of YES.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor of ~15% (Laplace-smoothed base rate adjusted for current refusal) updated downward by strong current-stance evidence. The gap between anchor and estimate is justified by the emphatic nature of his current refusal and the strict resolution criteria. Not hedging toward 50% — the evidence is quite directional here.
input.key_uncertainties: The biggest uncertainty is whether some dramatic development in the campaign (e.g., one candidate making a dramatic concession to Fajardo's decálogo, or a crisis threatening democratic institutions) could force Fajardo's hand. Also uncertain: whether his "I'll decide if I announce my vote" statement leaves more room than his other statements suggest.
input.tool_audit: Web search: Highly useful, returned detailed articles from El Colombiano, La FM, Pulzo, Infobae with direct Fajardo quotes. Fetch URL: Worked well for extracting key details from articles. Research sub-agent: Had tool access issues (couldn't search web) but provided good historical background from training data. Edelman analysis fetch: Very useful for understanding kingmaker dynamics. Wikipedia API data: Confirmed first-round results.
input.update_triggers: 1. Fajardo scheduling a meeting with either campaign. 2. One of the runoff candidates publicly adopting Fajardo's decálogo. 3. A democratic crisis (coup threat, election integrity concerns) that could force centrist alignment. 4. Fajardo's coalition members breaking ranks and endorsing, creating pressure.
input.process_reflection: The research sub-agent had tool availability issues, which meant I needed to do direct web searches myself. This is a somewhat common pattern — the research agents sometimes can't access the same tools. Direct web search + fetch_url was the most productive combination here. The question is well-structured with clear resolution criteria, making analysis straightforward. Spanish-language search was valuable for getting primary source coverage of Fajardo's statements.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Resolution Criteria
Resolution criteria were successfully retrieved via `get_metaculus_questions` (line 36-56 of trace). The factors and assessment engage thoroughly with the specific terms: unconditional named endorsement required, conditional statements don't count, blank vote advocacy resolves NO, 9-day window (June 5-14). No resolution misalignment.

### Hallucinated Evidence Check
**Factor 1 (historical pattern, 0/2 endorsements):** The claim that Fajardo cast blank votes in both 2018 and 2022 is supported by the question's own background info and one research sub-agent entry. However, a second research sub-agent entry claims Fajardo "eventually gave qualified, tepid support to Petro" in 2022. The agent noticed this contradiction (trace line 794) and resolved it by relying on the question description's account. The Metaculus question background explicitly states "Fajardo cast a blank vote in the 2018 and 2022 runoffs." Both sub-agent entries are from training data without web access, and the question author presumably researched this. The 0/2 claim is defensible given the resolution criteria — even if there was tepid support in 2022, it was likely not the kind of unconditional named endorsement this question requires. Not flagging as hallucination.

**Factor 2 (current emphatic refusal):** Well-grounded in multiple fetched articles. Direct quotes: "No estoy negociando nada con nadie" (El Colombiano), "los votos no son de los dirigentes" (El Colombiano), "El voto en blanco es una opción legítima" (La FM), "Voy a votar por quien yo quiera... Y yo veré si anuncio mi voto o no lo anuncio" (Pulzo/Blu Radio). Trace confirms these via fetch_url results from El Colombiano, La FM, and Pulzo.

**Factor 3 (principled identity framing):** Supported by the fetched articles showing Fajardo frames the decálogo as principles, not negotiation tools: "Yo no estoy presentando estos puntos para negociar a nadie" (El Colombiano). Grounded.

**Factor 4 (close margin/pressure):** The 2.84pp margin is confirmed by Edelman analysis and Wikipedia (43.74% vs 40.90%). The kingmaker narrative is confirmed by Edelman's characterization of the Valencia-Fajardo bloc as "potentially pivotal." Grounded.

**Factor 5 (polarized matchup):** Confirmed by multiple sources describing a left-right matchup between De la Espriella and Cepeda. However, the claim about De la Espriella having "authoritarian tendencies" and discussing "constitutional reform" appears only in the counterargument, not in the factors themselves, and I didn't find specific trace support for this characterization. Since it's in the counterargument (not a scored factor), this doesn't affect the forecast.

**Factor 6 (9-day window):** Straightforward calendar math. The question closes June 5 but resolves June 14. Correct.

**Factor 7 (strict fine print):** Accurately summarizes the resolution criteria. Grounded.

### Double-Counting Check
Factors 1 (historical pattern) and 3 (principled identity) draw on related evidence — Fajardo's past behavior and his self-positioning. However, they capture different dimensions: Factor 1 is the empirical base rate (what he did), Factor 3 is about his psychological/branding commitment (why he does it). Borderline but not flagging since they have distinct causal mechanisms.

Factors 6 (short time window) and 7 (strict fine print) both serve to constrain the probability of YES, but through different mechanisms (time pressure vs. definitional strictness). Acceptable.

### Wrong-Direction Factor Check
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions: negative logits for factors arguing against endorsement, positive logits for factors creating pressure toward endorsement. No wrong-direction factors.

### Contradictory Assessment Check
The assessment reaches a ~9% YES conclusion. The factors collectively have a strong negative logit sum (approximately -4.3), which the agent's reflection noted implies ~1.7%. The agent then adjusted upward to 9% to account for "tail scenarios the factor framework doesn't capture." This is a reasonable adjustment — 1.7% is arguably too low for a 9-day window in a dynamic political situation. The 9% estimate is directionally consistent with the overwhelmingly negative evidence. No contradiction.

### Anchor Divergence Check
Anchor is set at ~15% (adjusted down from 25% Laplace-smoothed). Final probability is 9%. The ~6pp departure from the anchor is modest and driven by the strong current-evidence factors (especially Factor 2, current emphatic refusal, with logit=-2.0 and 0.95 confidence). No concern.

### Weak Counterargument Check
The counterargument is substantive: it identifies the razor-thin margin as genuinely different from prior elections, notes Fajardo's deliberate ambiguity ("I'll decide whether to announce my vote or not"), identifies the decálogo as a potential off-ramp, and mentions coalition pressure from Jennifer Pedraza. It also specifies concrete trigger events that would change the estimate. This is a genuinely adversarial argument, not a token gesture.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment Check
Confidence is 0.65, which is appropriate given: strong current evidence (multiple direct quotes from named sources), decent historical data (2 prior cycles), but uncertainty about a 9-day future window with active political dynamics. 0.65 is well-calibrated.

### Probability Assessment
Based on the evidence:
- Strong historical base rate: 0/2 endorsements (or 1/2 if we credit the "tepid Petro support" in 2022 — but under these strict criteria it likely wouldn't count)
- Current emphatic, categorical, multi-statement refusal as of June 3-5
- Decálogo explicitly framed as non-negotiable principles
- Strict resolution criteria that filter out conditional/partial statements
- 9-day window in which something could change

My independent estimate: ~8-12% seems right. The dominant evidence is strongly against, but there's genuine uncertainty over a 9-day window with intense political pressure. 9% is within my range and well-reasoned.

### Tool Audit Check
The agent's tool audit narrative states the research sub-agent "Had tool access issues (couldn't search web)" — this is confirmed by the sub-agent output which says "all web search and fetch tools were unavailable." The tool metrics show 0% error rate for the research tool because the research call itself succeeded (returned results), even though the sub-agent couldn't access web tools internally. The audit also mentions Wikipedia API confirming first-round results; the metrics show 25% errors (1/4 calls), but the audit doesn't claim zero failures for Wikipedia. No significant discrepancy.

### Worldview Consistency Check
The worldview research entries are consistent with the factors. The research file `sergio-fajardo-blank-vote-2018-2022` confirms the blank vote pattern. The `sergio-fajardo-endorsement-colombia-2026` entry notes tool unavailability but provides background consistent with the forecast. No forecasts directory exists for cross-question consistency checks. No contradictions found.

### Verdict
No errors that would change the forecast. The evidence is well-grounded, the probability is reasonable, factors are correctly signed, and the assessment is consistent. Minor note: the 2022 historical record has conflicting accounts (blank vote vs. tepid Petro support), but the agent noticed this and resolved it appropriately. Approve.
